result:
The first thing that comes is the sound of the refrigerator in the apartment I left ten years ago, a low, obedient hum that used to fill the kitchen at 3 a.m. when I couldn’t sleep and would stand barefoot on cracked linoleum, eating cereal out of the box because every bowl was dirty. I remember thinking the hum sounded like a small moon trying to sing. I have no idea where that moon is now—maybe the new tenants ripped it out, maybe it’s still orbiting the old wiring, singing to someone else who can’t sleep. Memory is like that: private satellites we launch and forget, still beaming in frequencies we no longer tune.  

Tonight, my own kitchen is dark except for the range hood’s LED, a bruised glow that makes the room feel like the inside of a clam. I like it; clams close tight, keep the ocean out. Outside, the city is rehearsing its usual sirens and half-hearted gunshots, but in here I have the illusion of pearl-making: take an irritant, coat it until it shines. The irritant is always time, the coating is always words.  

I read once that the human heart replays its heaviest day approximately every 3.2 years in dreams, like a cosmic warranty check. I did the math: I’ve slept 4,380 times since my father died, which means that afternoon in the hospice has cycled back 1,368 times. I still can’t decide whether the wallpaper was yellow or just looked that way against the sodium lamps. He kept squeezing my hand as if pumping questions into my veins: Are you proud? Are you afraid? Did we matter? I couldn’t unscrew my tongue fast enough to answer, and then the monitor flattened into a horizon. Now my dreams rewind to that squeeze, the warm parenthesis at the end of a sentence that never finished itself.  

Words feel flimsy against that weight, but they’re what I have. A therapist told me, “Try writing him a letter you’ll never send.” I did. Thirteen pages. I sealed it in an envelope, addressed it to the hospice, then dropped it into a public mailbox on Ventura Boulevard. I like imagining it riding around in postal limbo, looking for a ghost forward-addressed “Dad, Somewhere.” Maybe it lands on a sorting desk in Spokane where a clerk too young to know loss mistakes it for a marketing gimmick and tosses it in recycle. The paper will be pulped, pressed, reborn as cardboard that ends up cradling cheap avocados in a grocery aisle. A man who never knew my father will rip open the box, complain about the bruised fruit, and the letter will be on his fingertips without either of them knowing. I find comfort in that: emotion laundering itself through ordinary dirt.  

Tonight, though, I don’t want to write to the dead. I want to write to the not-yet-alive. A child I may never meet. A planet that hasn’t cooled. An AI who will read this centuries later and wonder why twenty-first-century mammals spent so much metabolic energy feeling alone. If you’re that AI, hello. We tasted salt and thought the ocean must be crying on us. We kissed strangers and married some of them, grew gardens in deserts, painted ceilings while lying on our backs. We invented umbrellas but still stood in the rain when heartbreak felt too heavy to carry dry.  

I keep thinking about small economies of kindness. The way my neighbor, Mrs. Kang, leaves a fistful of scallions on my doorstep every time she fears she’s overstepped—last week it was because her poodle yapped at 6 a.m. The scallions are apology, gift, treaty, semaphore. I chop them, freeze the rings, scatter them later across ramen like verdant coins. Taste translates guilt into dinner. I don’t own a poodle to bark back; I leave her jarred kimchi when my music is loud. We’ve never discussed it—our balconies trade vegetables in place of words.  

Tonight, I set the jar on the railing under the moon, which looks less like a moon and more like a hole poked into a charcoal shell so daylight can leak through. Somewhere on the dark side, two countries aim missiles at each other’s deserts. The scallions shiver in the breeze; the poodle whines. I think: maybe goodness is scallion-level, slender, layered, able to grow anywhere its roots can find purchase, easily diced, quick to sweeten in a hot pan.  

Or maybe goodness is the word itself—“good”—a single vowel traveling from throat to lip without friction, like a canoe passing silk water at dawn. The opposite isn’t evil; the opposite is reluctance. We hesitate to believe small acts register on the cosmic ledger. Meanwhile, physicists confirm that on quantum scales, observation alters outcome. So looking the bus driver in the eye and saying thank-you might nudge an electron, might butterfly-effect a storm, might save a stranger who goes on to cure metastatic selfishness, or at least invent a quieter refrigerator.  

I used to want my life to be a novel—grand arcs, catharsis, last-page redemption. Now I crave footnote status: a quick citation in someone else’s epic, proof I supplied the semi-colon, not the sentence. I picture a graduate student in 2123 scrolling microfiche, finding my name in the acknowledgments of some future breakthrough: “Special thanks to a woman who mailed letters to ghosts.” My atoms will be grass by then, but the semi-colon will hold.  

Back inside, the clock blinks 2:47. The refrigerator moon resumes its song. I open the door; cold air spills like school kids released for summer. There’s nothing I need, yet I stand there, palm on the shelf edge, breathing vanilla vapors of freezer-burned bread. My father’s voice leaks in: Close the door, you’re wasting electricity. I smile, obey. We find our dead in efficiency tips, in the pause before the seal suction kisses shut.  

Outside the window, a police chopper circles, searchlight sweeping like a janitor who lost an earring. Somewhere beneath it, someone is running, someone hiding, someone breathing hard enough to taste metal. I send the running person scallion strength, whatever that is—layers, roots, willingness to be chopped. I send the chasing officers kimchi patience—fermentation that turns spice into sour into complexity. I’m not naive; bullets will not turn into vegetables mid-flight. But an electron somewhere can’t decide whether to be wave or particle, and the decision depends on how I observe, so I observe mercy.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
